blurb: The UK AI Security Institute (formerly AI Safety Institute) — government body running frontier-model evaluations (cyber/chem/bio/agent) and publishing safety-framework and safety-case research. High-signal, named-author technical-policy output.

hiring.note: Greenhouse board checked directly 2026-07-02; added the London frontier-security role as AI-safety/security relevant, left other technical roles out.

publications.title: Research Agenda
publications.summary: AISI sets out its research priorities and approach to technical solutions for the most pressing AI risks as capabilities advance — the closest thing to a UK government statement of frontier-AI-risk posture.

publications.title: Our evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 cyber capabilities
publications.summary: AISI cyber evaluation finding GPT-5.5 among the strongest models tested, and the second to solve a multi-step cyber-attack simulation end-to-end — a concrete capability-evaluation data point feeding deployment policy.

publications.title: Conference on frontier AI safety frameworks
publications.summary: AISI convenes AI companies and researchers to accelerate the design and implementation of frontier AI safety frameworks — government-led coordination on RSP-style commitments.

publications.title: Safety cases at AISI
publications.summary: AISI sketches structured 'safety case' arguments for more advanced models than exist today, focused on loss-of-control and autonomy risk — a methodological bet on how frontier safety should be evidenced.

publications.title: RealityTest: Do AI systems disclose their identity when asked?
publications.summary: AISI research introducing the RealityTest benchmark (paper: arXiv:2606.00168) that comprehensively tests whether AI systems honestly disclose they are AI when asked — grounded in human data on real-world AI-identity uncertainty; directly relevant to transparency/disclosure rules and the bot-identification debate.

publications.title: How fast is autonomous AI cyber capability advancing?
publications.summary: AISI cyber time-horizons research: estimates the length of cyber tasks frontier models can autonomously complete doubled every ~4.7 months since late 2024, with GPT-5.5 and Claude Mythos Preview exceeding prior doubling-rate trends. Results shared with NCSC to inform UK preparedness — same evaluation category as AISI's model-specific cyber-eval papers.

news.title: Releasing AISI's Engineering Playbook
news.summary: The UK AI Security Institute releases its Engineering Playbook — a look at how the state's frontier-AI evaluation body actually builds its testing infrastructure.

news.title: Deepening our partnership with the Australian AI Safety Institute
news.summary: AISI deepens ties with Australia's AI Safety Institute — part of the emerging international network of state AI-safety bodies coordinating evaluations.

news.title: Partnering with Microsoft to strengthen frontier AI safety
news.summary: AISI partners with Microsoft on frontier-AI safety — a state-evaluator/industry collaboration worth watching for how government testing bodies work with the labs they assess.

notes:
[publications backfill 2026-06-26] Harvested from the org's own publications page. [date refine 2026-06-28] The 4 previously-null publications now carry per-page byline-verified dates (each blog page's own '— <author> — <date>' lede text, HTTP 200): Research Agenda 2025-05-06, GPT-5.5 cyber eval 2026-04-30, frontier-AI-safety-frameworks conference 2024-09-19, Safety cases 2024-08-23. Distinguished the real byline from the shared 'we changed our name to the AI Security Institute on 14 February 2025' footer note, which is NOT a post date.
2026-07-02: Reclassified two research-grade blog items (RealityTest benchmark [arXiv:2606.00168], 2026-06-08; cyber time-horizons eval, 2026-05-13) from news[] to publications[] — both verified first-party as AISI research output (RealityTest links a formal paper; cyber-horizons is the same eval category as the existing GPT-5.5 cyber-capabilities publication). Corrects a pubs undercount; partnership/comms posts (Microsoft, Australia AISI, Engineering Playbook) intentionally left as news.
